elements.sent-2411-004: We propose a generalization of the theory of magnetic Skyrmions in chiral magnets in two dimensions to a higher-dimensional theory with magnetic Skyrmions in three dimensions and an $S^3$ target space, requiring a 4-dimensional magnetization vector.
elements.sent-2411-005: A physical realization of our theory necessitates the use of a synthetic dimension, recently promoted and realized in condensed matter physics.
elements.sent-2411-006: In the simplest incarnation of the theory, we find a Skyrmion and a sphaleron -- the latter being an unstable soliton.
elements.sent-2411-007: Including also the Skyrme term in theory enriches the spectrum to a small metastable Skyrmion, an unstable sphaleron and a large stable Skyrmion.
elements.sent-2411-008: The magnetic Skyrmion is gaining recognition as a prominent soliton in 2-dimensional magnetic physics, particularly in the context of chiral magnets and thin films~\cite{1989JETP...68..101B,1995JETPL..62..247B,Nagaosa2013,doi:10.1126/science.1166767,Yu2010 , as well as spintronics and quantum technologies~\cite{fert2017magnetic, everschor2018perspective, luo2021skyrmion, psaroudaki2023skyrmion .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: The name Skyrmion comes from the 3-dimensional soliton in the Skyrme model, proposed by T.~Skyrme in the 1960s as a model of the nucleus~\cite{skyrme1961non,skyrme1962unified , further validated as a low-energy effective field theory of QCD at large $N_c$ (number of colors) by E.~Witten~\cite{Witten:1983tw,Witten:1983tx and later used as a validation of the Sakai-Sugimoto model as evidence that the model really describes QCD at low energies~\cite{Sakai:2004cn .}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The Skyrmion in 2 or 3 dimensions is a texture soliton, which differs from a defect-type soliton by mapping the entire 2- or 3-dimensional space to the target space.
elements.sent-2411-011: In particular, a magnetic Skyrmion in two dimensions is mapping the entire plane to an $S^2$ target space, with the latter being the normalized magnetization vector $\bm{n =(n^1,n^2,n^3)$ such that $\bm{n}\cdot\bm{n =1$~\cite{roessler2006spontaneous, Rossler:2010st, Ezawa:2010uy, Banerjee:2014hna, melcher2014chiral, Rybakov:2018bxt, schroers2019gauged, barton2020magnetic, kuchkin2020magnetic, ross2021skyrmion, hill2021chiral, schroers2021solvable, Amari:2022boe, Hanada:2023lnm .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: It differs drastically from the 3-dimensional Skyrmion by the fact that: 1) it is stabilized by a lower-dimensional operator (i.e.~less derivatives than number of spatial dimensions) and 2) it is chiral.
elements.sent-2411-013: The latter implies that magnetic Skyrmions stably exist, but no anti-Skyrmions exist.
elements.sent-2411-014: This is different from 3-dimensional Skyrmions, where a higher-dimensional operator (i.e.~more derivatives than number of spatial dimensions) is used to stabilize the soliton -- called a Skyrmion -- against collapse.
elements.sent-2411-015: Indeed the higher-derivative (or smaller-derivative) terms are a way to avoid Derrick's theorem \cite{Derrick:1964ww .}
elements.sent-2411-016: In this paper, we consider the generalization of the 2-dimensional magnetic Skyrmion with target space $S^2$ to a 3-dimensional magnetic Skyrmion with target space $S^3$.\footnote{A preliminary investigation started in \cite{menta2023magnetic .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Since the unit magnetization vector in three dimensions gives rise to $S^2$, we need a unit magnetization vector in four dimensions.\footnote{We use the notation ``magnetization vector'' for the 4-dimensional generalization of the 3-dimension magnetization vector, although it is physically different.}
elements.sent-2411-018: For example, it is not possible to write $\bm{B}\cdot\bm{n}$, since the 3-dimensional magnetic field has 3 components and a would-be 4-dimensional magnetic field cannot be dualized to a vector (the 4-dimensional Hodge dual of a 2-form is also a 2-form).
elements.sent-2411-019: We will henceforth denote the construct as a higher-dimensional magnetic Skyrmion.
elements.sent-2411-020: The physical realization of a 3-dimensional magnetic Skyrmion would necessitate the incorporation of a synthetic (extra) dimension, as has been demonstrated in a number of physical systems -- e.g.~see Refs.~\cite{Boada2012,Celi2014,Ozawa2015,Price2015,Yuan2018,Ozawa2019,Wang2020,Price2022,Hazzard2023,Arguello-Luengo2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Until now, the 3-dimensional counterpart of 2D magnetic Skyrmions were magnetic Hopfions, 3-dimensional solitons living in a magnetization vector that is also living in three dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-022: Recent numerical computations \cite{sutcliffe2017skyrmion,sutcliffe2018hopfions,sutcliffe2018hopfionsReview,tai2018static,liu2018binding suggest that similar objects may exist at the nanoscale in frustrated and chiral magnets.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Micrometer-sized Hopfions have recently been created and observed experimentally in chiral ferromagnetic fluids \cite{ackerman2017static,kent2021creation .}
elements.sent-2411-024: The 3D magnetic Skyrmions which we propose, instead, have $S^3$ as a target space, the same as used in the standard Skyrme model in three dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-025: In this paper, we concentrate on the spherically symmetric case which enjoys $\SO(3)$ invariance.
elements.sent-2411-026: We take the generalized Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction (DMI)~\cite{dzyaloshinsky1958thermodynamic, moriya1960anisotropic, moriya1960new to have only one derivative of the magnetization vector and a single component of the same vector.}
elements.sent-2411-027: In four dimensions, this means that such a term must be contracted with a tensor that possesses two $\SO(4)$-indices and a single spatial index: we denote such a tensor by $\Theta$.
elements.sent-2411-028: We further impose $\SO(3)$ invariance, where we are locking spatial rotations with an $\SO(3)\subset\SO(4)$ rotation, in order to obtain a spherically symmetric soliton.
elements.sent-2411-029: This procedure leads to two different invariant structures in the $\Theta$ tensor.
elements.sent-2411-030: However, only one of these two structures remains nonvanishing when a spherically symmetric Ansatz is imposed.
elements.sent-2411-031: We furthermore show that both the Bloch-type and the N\'eel-type DMI generalizations lead to the same term when $\SO(3)$ invariance is imposed.
elements.sent-2411-032: A special feature of this higher-dimensional magnetic Skyrme model, is that it has multiple solutions.
elements.sent-2411-033: Indeed in its simplest incarnation, it has a Skyrmion and a sphaleron \cite{manton2019inevitability , with the latter being unstable and much smaller than the Skyrmion.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Finally, we further extend the model by including the standard $S^3$ Skyrme term, which further enriches the phase diagram of the model, by engendering the model with a small metastable Skyrmion, an unstable sphaleron and a stable Skyrmion -- at least in a finite region of the model's parameter space.
elements.sent-2411-035: The paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-036: In Sec.~\ref{sec:3D , we consider the minimal generalization of 2D magnetic Skyrmions to 3D magnetic Skyrmions with a 4-dimensional magnetization vector.}
elements.sent-2411-037: In particular, in Sec.~\ref{sec:genDM we consider the generalizations of the DM term and its reductions due to symmetry considerations.}
elements.sent-2411-038: In Sec.~\ref{sec:sph_DM_Sk , we then study spherically symmetric Skyrmions numerically and consider the limit of the kinetic term being absent, leading to the so-called restricted model (analogous to other Skyrme-type models).}
elements.sent-2411-039: In Sec.~\ref{sec:sphalerons , we then turn to unstable solitons -- the sphalerons of the theory -- which are the smaller ones and harder to find.}
elements.sent-2411-040: In Sec.~\ref{sec:magSkHopf , we describe a limit with an additional potential, in which the model eventually reduces to a chiral magnetic Hopfion model.}
elements.sent-2411-041: In Sec.~\ref{sec:hybrid , we then consider including the Skyrme term which enriches the theory with an extra and tiny Skyrmion that is only metastable.}
elements.sent-2411-042: In Sec.~\ref{sec:synthetic , we discuss the possibility of using a synthetic dimension to realize our theory in the real world.}
elements.sent-2411-043: We conclude in Sec.~\ref{sec:conclusion with a discussion and conclusion.}

elements.section-2411-004: Physical realization of higher-dimensional magnetic solitons
elements.sent-2411-237: In the realm of quantum simulations for condensed matter phenomena, the idea of ``synthetic dimensions'' \cite{Boada2012,Hazzard2023 has recently become a valuable tool for replicating effects like topological phases of matter \cite{Ozawa2019,Arguello-Luengo2024 , which arise in higher-dimensional physical systems.}}
elements.sent-2411-238: This concept revolves around connecting certain degrees of freedom, such as various internal states of atoms \cite{Price2015,Ozawa2015,Price2022 or photons \cite{Yuan2018 , to emulate the movement of a particle along an additional spatial dimension.}}
elements.sent-2411-239: Lattice Hamiltonians allow for the simulation of higher-dimensional topological models in lower-dimensional systems, though they require reinterpretation of internal degrees of freedom to emulate an extra spatial dimension.
elements.sent-2411-240: Traditional methods, like embedding higher-dimensional lattices or using topological pumps, are challenging or limited in capturing full dynamics.
elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{synthetic.pdf} \caption{Synthetic dimensions transform internal states or intrinsic properties into an effective spatial dimension. (a) A 2D discrete lattice model includes one real spatial dimension and one synthetic dimension composed of spin states. Hopping along the real dimension (solid lines) represents actual particle motion, while hopping along the synthetic dimension (dashed lines) corresponds to externally induced transitions between spin states. (b) A 4D hypercubic lattice illustrates a combination of real and synthetic spatial dimensions, showcasing how higher-dimensional structures can emerge in lower-dimensional systems. } \label{fig:synthetic}
elements.sent-2411-241: In contrast, synthetic dimensions offer an innovative path for lower-dimensional systems to explore higher-dimensional physics.
elements.sent-2411-242: The key idea is to take a set of unconnected internal states within a system and re-imagine them as lattice sites along a spatial dimension.
elements.sent-2411-243: By introducing specific external couplings, particles can effectively ``hop'' along this synthetic dimension, mimicking movement across a real lattice \cite{Celi2014 as shown pictorially in Fig.~\ref{fig:synthetic .}}
elements.sent-2411-244: What remains to be specified is the nature of the internal states of the system which will effectively act as an extra spatial dimension.
elements.sent-2411-245: For magnetic Hopfions or Skyrmions, the spin-orbit coupling (SOC) is especially promising because it influences chirality and texture stability, giving rise to complex configurations that could correspond to synthetic 4D behavior.
elements.sent-2411-246: We leave the search for the exact physical system that could be a candidate for providing the synthetic dimension for our higher-dimensional version of the DM interaction for future work.
elements.figure-2411-016: \includegraphics[width=0.6\linewidth]{{skyrmion_xyw_projection.pdf}} \caption{Illustration of the magnetization vector in an example where the physical dimensions are mapped as $\bm{n}=(n^x,n^y,n^{\rm synth},n^z)$, with $n^{\rm synth}$ representing the component of the would-be magnetization vector in the synthetic dimension. } \label{fig:synth_proj}
elements.sent-2411-247: In Fig.~\ref{fig:synth_proj we illustrate an example of a Skyrmion when the $n^3$ element of the 4-dimensional magnetization vector correspond to the synthetic dimension.}
elements.sent-2411-248: In this paper, we have generalized the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction for a 3D magnetization vector to a 4D magnetization vector -- hence a higher-dimensional version of the DMI.
elements.sent-2411-249: Using the property that it is first order in derivatives and must be nonvanishing, we deduced that it must be contracted with a tensor that is antisymmetric in $\Og(4)$-indices and bears a 3D spatial index.
elements.sent-2411-250: Considering furthermore the simplified case of $\SO(3)$ invariance, we are able to reduce the DM term to just two invariant structures, that we denote the $\alpha$ and $\beta$ parts.
elements.sent-2411-251: It turns out that the $\beta$-part is irrelevant for the spherically symmetric case and only the $\alpha$-part stabilizes the solitons.
elements.sent-2411-252: Due to the higher dimension of space, there are in fact two solitons with just the DMI and a potential: the Skyrmion and a sphaleron, where the latter is an unstable and smaller solution of the model.
elements.sent-2411-253: Upon reduction to the Hopfion model, the $\beta$-part nevertheless plays an important role.
elements.sent-2411-254: Finally, we consider including the Skyrme term, thus considering a magnetic-QCD hybrid Skyrme model that turns out to host a large and a small Skyrmion as well as a sphaleron -- at least in a small region of parameter space of the model.
elements.sent-2411-255: The large Skyrmion is stable, the small Skyrmion is metastable and the sphaleron is unstable.
elements.sent-2411-256: The phase diagram is quite rich and there exist lines where the different types of solutions coalesce and in the remaining part of the parameter space of the model, there exists only one solution -- the Skyrmion.
elements.sent-2411-257: We have contemplated that by using a synthetic extra dimension, it would be possible to construct our higher-dimensional magnetic Skyrmion or perhaps the hybrid Skyrmion in a physically realizable condensed matter system.
elements.sent-2411-258: The details of whether the exact Hamiltonian corresponding to our model can be constructed, still needs to be worked out.
elements.sent-2411-259: We have focused on spherically symmetric solitons in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-260: Using the spherically symmetric hedgehog Ansatz, we found that $\beta$-part of the $\SO(3)$-invariant DM term \eqref{eq:Theta_inv_standard vanishes and so we effectively only used the $\alpha$-part of the $\SO(3)$-invariant DM term.}
elements.sent-2411-261: Deformed solitons, however, may feel the presence of the $\beta$-part of the DM term, which in principle could lower the mass of the soliton.
elements.sent-2411-262: We leave the exploration of such a possibility for future work.
elements.sent-2411-263: We also considered a connection to the Hopfion in chiral magnets, and although there exists a higher-dimensional DM term and a potential with a limit that connects to the Hopfion model, the interpolation is not expected to be smooth.
elements.sent-2411-264: That is, the topology changes, sending the masses of the nontrivial $\pi_3(S^3)$ solitons to infinity and the nontrivial $\pi_3(S^2)$ solitons appear first as metastable and then finally become stable at the end point of the limit.
elements.sent-2411-265: A smooth interpolation is thus desirable and will be left for future work.
elements.sent-2411-266: In summary, this work presents an initial study of a generalized model for higher-dimensional magnetic Skyrmions, revealing rich solitonic physics.
